ontology.foodex2_description: Flat bread (unleavened or leavened); closest parent category for naan in FoodEx2 hierarchy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for naan specifically; A000F (flat bread) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plain naan as served in an Indian restaurant, approximately 90 g, equivalent to one oval flatbread piece.

nutrient_source.title: Naan bread, plain — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 18960)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain naan bread used as primary nutrient reference. Values represent plain naan without additional toppings or fillings. ICMR-NIN Indian food composition data corroborates the macronutrient profile for plain naan (maida-based flatbread).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain naan bread GI reported as approximately 71 (high GI) in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (71 × 45.0) / 100 ≈ 32.0, using 90 g serving with 50.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g (45.0 g per 90 g serving).

hypertension.guidance: Plain naan contains approximately 536 mg sodium per 100 g, contributing meaningfully to daily sodium intake; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one small piece and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as salted butter or heavily spiced gravies.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 71) and high refined carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain naan is made from refined wheat flour (maida) and carries a high GI of 71 with a glycemic load of approximately 32 per standard serving, making it a significant postprandial glucose-raising food; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion to half a naan, pair with high-fibre vegetables or legumes, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate content and moderate saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain naan provides moderate saturated fat (1.1 g per 100 g) and is made from refined flour, which may contribute to unfavourable lipid profiles when consumed in excess; individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer whole-wheat roti or smaller portions of naan and limit frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plain naan is a traditional leavened Indian flatbread made primarily from refined wheat flour (maida), yogurt, yeast or baking powder, salt, and water. It is characteristically baked in a tandoor (clay oven) at very high temperatures, producing a soft, slightly chewy interior with characteristic charred bubbles on the surface and a mild, slightly tangy flavour from the yogurt fermentation. Plain naan contains no stuffing or flavouring beyond the base dough, distinguishing it from garlic naan, peshwari naan, or keema naan variants. Nutritionally, plain naan provides approximately 272 kcal per 100 g, with 50 g carbohydrate, 9 g protein, and 4 g fat. It is a high-GI food (GI ≈ 71) due to its refined flour base, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is notable at 536 mg per 100 g. In Singapore, plain naan is widely served in Indian restaurants and hawker stalls as an accompaniment to curries, dals, and tandoori dishes. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains gluten and dairy. Clinically, it is classified as a high-glycaemic staple and should be consumed in moderation by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those following a low-GI dietary pattern.
common_user_queries: How many calories in plain naan?

ingredient_synonyms.atta: whole wheat flour (not used in plain naan)

confidence_reason: Plain naan is a well-documented food in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy with an exact entry. GI value is confirmed in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Nutrient values are consistent across USDA and ICMR-NIN reference data for maida-based flatbread.
notes: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (plain naan, NDB 18960) and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian food composition data. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA source for plain naan). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero; plain naan contains no vitamin C-bearing ingredients). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero; plain naan is a plant-based bread product with no animal-derived B12 source beyond trace dairy). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA source; plain naan is not a vitamin D source). Serving size of 90 g reflects a standard single restaurant naan piece. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (71 × 45.0) / 100 = 31.95, rounded to 32.0. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimates from USDA SR Legacy fat fraction data. Sodium value (536 mg/100 g) reflects typical commercial/restaurant naan; home-prepared naan may have lower sodium. This record covers plain naan only; garlic, peshwari, and stuffed naan variants require separate records. Indian cuisine multilingual names provided: Chinese (原味馕 / 原味饢), Malay (Naan Kosong), Tamil (சாதா நான்), Hindi (सादा नान). Romanized Hindi: Saada Naan.
